questions.question: What is the primary purpose of JUnit in Java projects?
options: Write and run automated unit tests
questions.explanation: JUnit is a unit-testing framework for Java. It lets you define test methods, assert expected outcomes, and run tests as part of build pipelines (Maven/Gradle) or the IDE.

questions.question: Which JUnit 5 annotation marks a method as a single test case?
questions.explanation: @Test (org.junit.jupiter.api.Test) identifies an executable test method. @BeforeEach/@AfterEach handle setup/teardown; @RunWith is JUnit 4 style.

questions.question: In JUnit 5, which annotation runs once before each test method in the class?
questions.explanation: @BeforeEach executes before every @Test. @BeforeAll runs once per class before any test (often for expensive shared setup).

questions.question: What does this assertion verify?
questions.explanation: assertEquals(expected, actual) checks value equality (using equals for objects). The optional message appears on failure.

questions.question: How many times will the test method run?
questions.explanation: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource(ints = {1, 2, 3}) runs the test once per provided value, so three executions.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of using @DisplayName on tests?
questions.explanation: @DisplayName lets you describe tests clearly in IDE and CI output instead of showing only method names like testUserLogin_failureCase.

questions.question: When should you prefer @Disabled on a test?
options: When the test must always run in CI
When a test is temporarily broken or not applicable
questions.explanation: @Disabled excludes a test from execution while keeping it in the codebase—useful for flaky tests under investigation or environment-specific cases.

questions.question: What exception-related assertion is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: assertThrows(ExpectedException.class, () -> service.doWork()) verifies that the lambda throws the specified exception type.

questions.question: What does assertTimeout do in JUnit 5?
options: Asserts two values are equal within a time window
Fails if the executable exceeds the given duration
questions.explanation: assertTimeout(Duration, Executable) fails the test if the code block runs longer than the limit—useful for performance regressions.

questions.question: In a typical Maven project, where do unit tests live?
questions.explanation: Convention places production code under src/main/java and tests under src/test/java, compiled and run by the surefire plugin.

questions.question: Which annotation groups related tests and can apply tags at class level in JUnit 5?
questions.explanation: @Nested creates inner test classes for hierarchical organization. @Tag filters tests; @Suite composes multiple test classes.

questions.question: What is the difference between unit tests and integration tests in backend practice?
options: Unit tests always use a database; integration tests do not
Unit tests isolate a small unit with mocks; integration tests wire real collaborators
Integration tests run only on Fridays
questions.explanation: Unit tests focus on one class or method with dependencies stubbed. Integration tests verify components together (e.g., Spring context, database).

questions.question: What will happen when this test runs (assuming list is empty)?
questions.explanation: assertFalse on an empty list returns true for isEmpty(), so the assertion fails because the condition is false.

questions.question: Why use assertAll in JUnit 5?
options: Run tests in parallel on all CPU cores
Report all assertion failures in a group instead of stopping at the first
questions.explanation: assertAll executes multiple assertions and reports every failure together—helpful when validating several fields of one object.

questions.question: What problem does Mockito solve in unit testing?
questions.explanation: Mockito lets you create mock objects that simulate collaborators, define behavior with when/thenReturn, and verify interactions.

questions.question: What does this line create?
options: A real UserRepository connected to H2
A spy of an existing bean
A static mock for all tests
questions.explanation: Mockito.mock(Class) or @Mock creates a proxy implementing the type; methods return defaults until stubbed.

questions.question: Which JUnit 5 extension initializes @Mock fields automatically?
questions.explanation: MockitoExtension (or @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)) injects mocks and handles strict stubbing in JUnit 5 tests.

questions.question: When findById(1L) is called on the mock, what does it return?
questions.explanation: when(...).thenReturn(...) stubs the method. Here findById(1L) returns Optional.of(user); other arguments use default (empty Optional).

questions.question: What does verify(repo).save(user) assert?
options: save was called exactly once with this user
questions.explanation: verify checks interaction: that save(user) was invoked on the mock. Use times(n), never(), etc. for finer control.

questions.question: What is the difference between mock and spy in Mockito?
options: Spy calls real methods by default; mock does not unless stubbed
questions.explanation: A spy wraps a real object and delegates to it unless a method is stubbed. A mock has no real implementation—all methods are stubbed or return defaults.

questions.question: What is wrong with stubbing a void method this way?
options: void methods use doNothing()/doThrow() with when or doAnswer
questions.explanation: For void methods use doThrow(...).when(mock).method() or doNothing().when(mock).method() because thenReturn does not apply to void.

questions.question: What does ArgumentMatchers.anyString() match?
options: Any String including null (with nullable matchers)
questions.explanation: anyString() matches any String argument when stubbing or verifying. Use nullable(Class) when null must be accepted explicitly.

questions.question: What does @InjectMocks do?
options: Injects Spring beans from application context
Creates an instance and injects @Mock/@Spy fields into it
questions.explanation: @InjectMocks constructs the class under test and tries to inject mocks into constructor/setters/fields—ideal for testing a service with mocked DAO.

questions.question: Why avoid mocking types you do not own (e.g., JDK or third-party concrete classes)?
options: It couples tests to implementation details and can break on upgrades; prefer wrapping or interfaces
Mocks are slower than real objects always
questions.explanation: Mocking concrete libraries you do not control is fragile. Prefer your own interfaces or adapters so tests stay stable.

questions.question: How many times was deleteAll called after this test logic?
questions.explanation: verify(repo, times(2)).deleteAll() passes only if deleteAll was invoked twice. The service code must call it twice.

questions.question: What role does Hibernate play in a Java application?
options: An ORM that maps Java objects to relational tables
questions.explanation: Hibernate implements JPA-style object-relational mapping: entities become rows, associations become foreign keys, and HQL/Criteria query objects.

questions.question: What is the Hibernate Session in classic API terms?
options: A single-threaded workspace between app and database for CRUD and queries
questions.explanation: Session (now often EntityManager in JPA) tracks persistent entities, manages transactions, and synchronizes state with the database.

questions.question: What does SessionFactory represent?
options: A thread-safe factory expensive to create; produces Sessions/EntityManagers
questions.explanation: SessionFactory is built once from configuration (XML or properties) and is heavy to create. It spawns lightweight Session instances per unit of work.

questions.question: Which configuration file is commonly used for Hibernate settings in older setups?
questions.explanation: hibernate.cfg.xml (native Hibernate) or META-INF/persistence.xml (JPA) define datasource, dialect, and mapping resources.

questions.question: What is a Hibernate dialect used for?
options: Generating SQL appropriate to the database vendor
questions.explanation: Dialect translates HQL/Criteria and DDL to vendor-specific SQL (MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.) and handles pagination syntax differences.

questions.question: What does hbm2ddl.auto=update typically do in development?
options: Adjusts schema to match mappings (adds columns/tables; risky in production)
questions.explanation: update compares mappings to schema and applies incremental DDL. Never rely on it blindly in production—use migrations (Flyway/Liquibase).

questions.question: How does Hibernate relate to JPA?
options: JPA is a specification; Hibernate is a popular implementation
questions.explanation: JPA defines annotations and EntityManager API. Hibernate implements JPA and adds proprietary features (e.g., @Formula, extra fetch modes).

questions.question: Which annotation marks a class as a JPA/Hibernate entity?
questions.explanation: @Entity (javax/jakarta.persistence.Entity) declares a persistent class. @Table optionally names the table; @Id marks primary key.

questions.question: What does @Id on a field indicate?
options: Primary key column for the entity
questions.explanation: @Id designates the primary key. Combine with @GeneratedValue for identity/sequence/table strategies.

questions.question: Which strategy generates numeric keys using database IDENTITY columns?
questions.explanation: IDENTITY uses auto-increment (MySQL AUTO_INCREMENT, SQL Server IDENTITY). SEQUENCE uses a separate sequence object common in Oracle/PostgreSQL.

questions.question: What does @Column(name = "email_addr") do?
options: Maps the field to column email_addr
questions.explanation: @Column customizes column name, length, nullable, unique constraints. Default name is field name unless overridden.

questions.question: What is the difference between transient and persistent entity state?
options: Transient is in memory only, not associated with a Session; persistent is managed and tracked
questions.explanation: New objects are transient until persisted (save). Persistent entities are attached to a persistence context; detached after Session closes.

questions.question: Which mapping describes a many-to-one association from Order to Customer?
questions.explanation: @ManyToOne places the foreign key on the owning side (Order table has customer_id). @OneToMany is the inverse on Customer.

questions.question: In @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department"), what does mappedBy mean?
options: This side owns the foreign key
The other entity owns the relationship; this is the inverse side
questions.explanation: mappedBy points to the field on the child that owns the FK. The child (@ManyToOne) side owns the association in bidirectional mappings.

questions.question: What does @JoinColumn specify?
options: Foreign key column details on the owning side
questions.explanation: @JoinColumn(name = "dept_id") defines FK column name and optional referencedColumnName for @ManyToOne/@OneToOne owning side.

questions.question: When should you use @Embedded and @Embeddable?
options: To map a value object (e.g., Address) into columns of the parent table
questions.explanation: @Embeddable types flatten into the parent table columns (street, city) without a separate Address table—good for composite value objects.

questions.question: What does @ManyToMany with @JoinTable define?
options: A join table linking two entities
questions.explanation: @JoinTable names the link table and join columns for both sides. Many-to-many often needs explicit ownership and fetch strategy.
